Okay, how to fire a leader. The reason I wanted to make a separate module for this is I feel that it's oftentimes the hardest fire for someone to make. I know I've done it myself and it's also where people get the most emotional and typically ask for more support so I felt like it made more sense to go a little bit more in depth, right? So the first thing that you want to do when you're firing a leader is a root cause analysis, okay? So at this point if you have leadership in your team then you should already have systems in place to ensure that you're hiring right people for jobs in general, okay? So it's not a systems failure 99% of the time it's typically a you failure and that's something that you have to own and you have to accept and most of the time it's one of these issues, okay? One, you did a poor job defining the position in the first place so your expectations were not their expectations, right? Two, you hired for lack of weakness rather than for strengths meaning the person was good, mediocre, fine, right? And so you're like well they're not bad so I'll put them in this role rather than they're really good at this role. Two, you hired for scale too soon meaning that you hired a leader before you actually needed one and before you were ready to let go of things. You hired for potential not reality so you hired for what you thought this person could become rather than who they are, right? You're like they have so much potential if I just train them, no. You hired for two generic of a position meaning that you might have hired someone who you know was an operations manager elsewhere in a completely different market business etc rather than hiring someone who has context as to your business and understands the industry as a whole. And then lastly you failed to integrate the person properly, you failed to elevate them from the team, you failed to delegate to them and you failed to really prop them up and give them the credit that they were looking for, okay? So the first thing you need to do is identify which of these things caused the issue in the first place, okay? What I want to know is that there are special case scenarios. If you are fast like you grew incredibly fast you've gone from 50 or maybe even 30 EFTs to now you're at 300, 400, okay? That would I guess fall under this scenario, okay? So it's really common that a leader who can manage a gym of 50 members cannot manage a gym at 250 or 400 rubbers, okay? Because running a gym of this size is a completely different job, okay? So it's honestly not the job that they were hired for, right? It's a different job to run 400 people than it is to run 50, okay? And this is not a failure on your part as a leader or on the leaders in general. This is just what happens when you scale and grow quickly, okay? So please don't attempt to avoid this, you just have to embrace it and accept it and the best thing you can do is be honest with people about it, okay? Second thing you need to do, you need to alert other leadership, okay? So remember as hard as it is to discuss this with other leaders, say your partner or whomever else it is in a leadership position on the team, it's harder to leave a failing leader in place who is slowly rotting your company. And that is the quickest way to lose respect from your team, right? So you need to have three goals in mind when you're discussing this with your leadership team, okay? So that's the people above this person. The first one is that you want to get their support and understanding for the task at hand. So you want to make sure that they understand the root cause. You take ownership in front of the rest of leadership because remember, everything you're doing, they are going to replicate later on down the road and so you need to set a good example. And then two, you have to understand that if they don't understand, they're going to lose confidence in you. If they feel like you're lying or you're not taking ownership, they will lose respect and confidence for you as a leader. Two, you need to get their input onto how to let this person go. So this person has been with you for a while or they have a ton of involvement with your clients and the rest of the team. You need to see them off with a grace and ensure that you leave off on a good note. And so that means one, inquire with your HR service or your legal counsel to seek what kind of separation package would make sense. You know, is it appropriate? They've been with you for three or four years. You need to give them severance. Is it appropriate? You give them an amount of weeks left to work. How do you want to handle this situation? And there's a lot of variables to weigh out, you know, how involved they are with clients, how involved they are with the team, their attitude in general. Do they have a bad attitude or do they continue to have a great attitude and it's just not a good fit? There's a lot of stuff to consider there. And then third is you want to preserve the reputation of the leader. So you have to remember the failure was a team effort. You cannot portray it any other way and you don't make yourself look good by talking down about this person. It actually makes you look worse to the rest of your team. The third thing that you need to do is prepare the conversation. Okay, so once you've identified one wrong and you informed other leadership, you need to fire this person as soon as possible. Okay, it's eating up all of your attention. They probably sense that something is off and now other people on the team know. So it's very important that you do this quickly. Okay, so this person will take this conversation to heart and they probably will remember it for a long time because people always remember getting fired. And so here is what you need to cover during that conversation. Okay, one, be clear on the reason. Okay, so you don't want to sugarcoat the reasoning. You need to be honest because you do owe it to someone who's dedicated a lot of time and effort to your company to know the truth. It doesn't mean you need to go into detail. Remember, I did cover like you don't have to go into in great detail, but you do need to give them the overarching reason. Two, use decisive language. So this isn't a negotiation. Oftentimes when you're firing a leader, most of the times you don't tell them you're not commanding them of anything. This is a time where you have to be very stern and say, I have decided that we're going to do this, not I think. Right. And then third is having the next steps prepared. Okay, so that means covering their payout, covering what happens next in regards to their termination, you know, covering how you're going to cover some of the projects they have ongoing and how you're going to let clients know so that they're not left wondering. You make it clear that they're not to communicate with people outside any of the clients regarding work, etc. That kind of thing. And then third is preparing the company communication. So this is the order that you need to alert the company. The first is their direct reports. So ideally what you will do is when you're firing that person, you have someone else in leadership talking to their direct reports explaining that that person is actually let go. If you can't do that, then you need to meet with their team immediately after you terminate them. Right. Second is letting your direct reports know. Okay, so you don't want anyone that reports to you to get caught off guard. That's the second person that you need to inform. And then third is the rest of the company. So I would highly recommend you do the same day. If not, do it the very next morning. The goal is to do it face to face or on a Zoom call. So during a huddle is when you would announce this, right. And then meanwhile, you want to make sure that you have a contingency plan in place when this happens. So you need to already have decided who's going to replace this person temporarily, how long will the replacement be there. So if someone just jumping in until you hire someone else, are you giving them a chance at the job? Explain the expectations, right. And then lastly, you need to remember that if you take on this role, you need to do an amazing job at it. Why? Because you need to show the team what it should look like. So if you're telling them that you fired someone for doing a poor job, and then you come in and do a poor job, they're going to assume that you didn't know what the frick you were doing. Okay, so remember, you need to excel at this.
